sections.label: Section 910b
sections.original: καὶ βωμολόχος .
sections.translation: And a dirty lickspittle .

sections.label: Section 911
sections.original: κρίνεσι στεφανοῖς .
sections.translation: You crown me with lilies .

sections.label: Section 911b
sections.original: καὶ πατραλοίας .
sections.translation: And a parricide .

sections.label: Section 912
sections.original: χρυσῷ πάττων μʼ οὐ γιγνώσκεις .
sections.translation: You don ’ t know that you are sprinkling me with gold .

sections.label: Section 913
sections.original: οὐ δῆτα πρὸ τοῦ γʼ , ἀλλὰ μολύβδῳ .
sections.translation: Certainly not so formerly , but with lead .

sections.label: Section 914
sections.original: νῦν δέ γε κόσμος τοῦτʼ ἐστὶν ἐμοί .
sections.translation: But now this is an ornament to me .

sections.label: Section 915
sections.original: θρασὺς εἶ πολλοῦ .
sections.translation: You are very impudent .

sections.label: Section 915b
sections.original: σὺ δέ γʼ ἀρχαῖος .
sections.translation: And you are antiquated .

sections.label: Section 916
sections.original: διὰ σὲ δὲ φοιτᾶν
sections.translation: And through you , no one of our youths is willing to go to school ; and you will be found out some time or other by the Athenians , what sort of doctrines you teach the simple-minded .

sections.label: Section 920
sections.original: αὐχμεῖς αἰσχρῶς .
sections.translation: You are shamefully squalid .

sections.label: Section 920b
sections.original: σὺ δέ γʼ εὖ πράττεις .
sections.translation: And you are prosperous . And yet formerly you were a beggar saying that you were the Mysian Telephus , and gnawing the maxims of Pandeletus out of your little wallet .

sections.label: Section 925
sections.original: ὤμοι σοφίας —
sections.translation: Oh , the wisdom —

sections.label: Section 925b
sections.original: ὤμοι μανίας —
sections.translation: Oh , the madness —

sections.label: Section 926
sections.original: ἧς ἐμνήσθης —
sections.translation: Which you have mentioned .

sections.label: Section 927
sections.original: τῆς σῆς , πόλεώς θʼ ἥτις σε τρέφει
sections.translation: And of your city , which supports you who ruin her youths .

sections.label: Section 929
sections.original: οὐχὶ διδάξεις τοῦτον Κρόνος ὤν .
sections.translation: You shan ’ t teach this youth , you old dotard .

sections.label: Section 930
sections.original: εἴπερ γʼ αὐτὸν σωθῆναι χρὴ
sections.translation: Yes , if he is to be saved , and not merely to practise loquacity .

sections.label: Section 932
sections.original: δεῦρʼ ἴθι , τοῦτον δʼ ἔα μαίνεσθαι .
sections.translation: Come hither , and leave him to rave .

sections.label: Section 933
sections.original: κλαύσει , τὴν χεῖρʼ ἢν ἐπιβάλλῃς .
sections.translation: You shall howl , if you lay your hand on him .

sections.label: Section 934
sections.original: παύσασθε μάχης καὶ λοιδορίας .
sections.translation: Cease from contention and railing .

sections.label: Section 935
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐπίδειξαι σύ τε τοὺς προτέρους
sections.translation: But show to us , you , what you used to teach the men of former times , and you , the new system of education ; in order that , having heard you disputing , he may decide and go to the school of one or the other .

sections.label: Section 939
sections.original: δρᾶν ταῦτʼ ἐθέλω .
sections.translation: I am willing to do so .

sections.label: Section 939b
sections.original: κἄγωγʼ ἐθέλω .
sections.translation: I also am willing .

sections.label: Section 940
sections.original: φέρε δὴ πότερος λέξει πρότερος ;
sections.translation: Come now , which of the two shall speak first ?

sections.label: Section 941
sections.original: τούτῳ δώσω ·
sections.translation: I will give him the precedence ; and then , from these things which he adduces , I will shoot him dead with new words and thoughts .

sections.label: Section 945
sections.original: τὸ τελευταῖον δʼ , ἢν ἀναγρύζῃ ,
sections.translation: And at last , if he mutter , he shall be destroyed , being stung in his whole face and his two eyes by my maxims , as if by bees .

sections.label: Section 949
sections.original: νῦν δείξετον τὼ πισύνω τοῖς περιδεξίοισι
sections.translation: Now the two , relying on very dexterous arguments and thoughts , and sententious maxims , will show which of them shall appear superior in argument .

sections.label: Section 955
sections.original: νῦν γὰρ ἅπας ἐνθάδε κίνδυνος ἀνεῖται σοφίας ,
sections.translation: For now the whole crisis of wisdom is here laid before them ; about which my friends have a very great contest .

sections.label: Section 959
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὦ πολλοῖς τοὺς πρεσβυτέρους ἤθεσι χρηστοῖς στεφανώσας ,
sections.translation: But do you , who adorned our elders with many virtuous manners , utter the voice in which you rejoice , and declare your nature .

sections.label: Section 961
sections.original: λέξω τοίνυν τὴν ἀρχαίαν παιδείαν ὡς διέκειτο ,
sections.translation: I will , therefore , describe the ancient system of education , how it was ordered , when I flourished in the advocacy of justice , and temperance was the fashion . In the first place it was incumbent that no one should hear the voice of a boy uttering a syllable ; and next , that those from the same quarter of the town should march in good order through the streets to the school of the harp-master ,

sections.label: Section 965
sections.original: τοὺς κωμήτας γυμνοὺς ἁθρόους , κεἰ κριμνώδη κατανείφοι .
sections.translation: naked , and in a body , even if it were to snow as thick as meal . Then again , their master would teach them , not sitting cross-legged , to learn by rote a song , either Παλλάδα περσέπολιν δεινὰν or τηλέπορόν τι βόαμα raising to a higher pitch the harmony which our fathers transmitted to us . But if any of them were to play the buffoon , or to turn any quavers ,

sections.label: Section 970
sections.original: οἵας οἱ νῦν τὰς κατὰ Φρῦνιν ταύτας τὰς δυσκολοκάμπτους ,
sections.translation: like these difficult turns the present artists make after the manner of Phrynis , he used to be thrashed , being beaten with many blows , as banishing the Muses . And it behooved the boys , while sitting in the school of the Gymnastic-master , to cover the thigh , so that they might exhibit nothing indecent to those outside ;

sections.label: Section 975
sections.original: εἶτʼ αὖ πάλιν αὖθις ἀνιστάμενον συμψῆσαι , καὶ προνοεῖσθαι
sections.translation: then again , after rising from the ground , to sweep the sand together , and to take care not to leave an impression of the person for their lovers . And no boy used in those days to anoint himself below the navel ; so that their bodies wore the appearance of blooming health . Nor used he to go to his lover , having made up his voice in an effeminate tone ,

sections.label: Section 980
sections.original: αὐτὸς ἑαυτὸν προαγωγεύων τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς ἐβάδιζεν ,
sections.translation: prostituting himself with his eyes . Nor used it to be allowed when one was dining to take the head of the radish , or to snatch from their seniors dill or parsley , or to eat fish , or to giggle , or to keep the legs crossed .

sections.label: Section 984
sections.original: ἀρχαῖά γε καὶ Διιπολιώδη καὶ τεττίγων ἀνάμεστα
sections.translation: Aye , antiquated and dipolia-like and full of grasshoppers , and of Cecydes , and of the Buphonian festival !

sections.label: Section 985b
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὖν ταῦτʼ ἐστὶν ἐκεῖνα ,
sections.translation: Yet certainly these are those principles by which my system of education nurtured the men who fought at Marathon . But you teach the men of the present day , so that I am choked , when at the Panathenaia a fellow , holding his shield before his person , neglects Tritogenia , when they ought to dance .

sections.label: Section 990
sections.original: πρὸς ταῦτʼ ὦ μειράκιον θαρρῶν ἐμὲ τὸν κρείττω λόγον αἱροῦ ·
sections.translation: Wherefore , O youth , choose with confidence , me , the better cause , and you will learn to hate the Agora , and to refrain from baths , and to be ashamed of what is disgraceful , and to be enraged if any one jeer you , and to rise up from seats before your seniors when they approach , and not to behave ill toward your parents , and to do nothing else

sections.label: Section 995
sections.original: αἰσχρὸν ποιεῖν , ὅτι τῆς αἰδοῦς μέλλεις τἄγαλμʼ ἀναπλάττειν ·
sections.translation: that is base , because you are to form in your mind an image of Modesty : and not to dart into the house of a dancing-woman , lest , while gaping after these things , being struck with an apple by a wanton , you should be damaged in your reputation : and not to contradict your father in anything ; nor by calling him Iapetus , to reproach him with the ills of age , by which you were reared in your infancy .

sections.label: Section 1000
sections.original: εἰ ταῦτʼ ὦ μειράκιον πείσει τούτῳ , νὴ τὸν Διόνυσον
sections.translation: If you shall believe him in this , O youth , by Bacchus , you will be like the sons of Hippocrates , and they will call you a booby .

sections.label: Section 1002
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὖν λιπαρός γε καὶ εὐανθὴς ἐν γυμνασίοις διατρίψεις ,
sections.translation: Yet certainly shall you spend your time in the gymnastic schools , sleek and blooming ; not chattering in the market-place rude jests , like the youths of the present day ; nor dragged into court for a petty suit , greedy , pettifogging , knavish ;

sections.label: Section 1005
sections.original: ἀλλʼ εἰς Ἀκαδήμειαν κατιὼν ὑπὸ ταῖς μορίαις ἀποθρέξει
sections.translation: but you shall descend to the Academy and run races beneath the sacred olives along with some modest compeer , crowned with white reeds , redolent of yew , and careless ease , of leaf-shedding white poplar , rejoicing in the season of spring , when the plane-tree whispers to the elm .

sections.label: Section 1009
sections.original: ἢν ταῦτα ποιῇς ἁγὼ φράζω ,
sections.translation: If you do these things which I say , and apply your mind to these , you will ever have a stout chest , a clear complexion , broad shoulders , a little tongue , large hips , little lewdness .

sections.label: Section 1015
sections.original: ἢν δʼ ἅπερ οἱ νῦν ἐπιτηδεύῃς ,
sections.translation: But if you practise what the youths of the present day do , you will have in the first place , a pallid complexion , small shoulders , a narrow chest , a large tongue , little hips , great lewdness ,

sections.label: Section 1020
sections.original: ψήφισμα μακρόν , καὶ σʼ ἀναπείσει
sections.translation: a long psephism ; and this deceiver will persuade you to consider everything that is base to be honourable , and what is honourable to be base ; and in addition to this , he will fill you with the lewdness of Antimachus .

sections.label: Section 1024
sections.original: ὦ καλλίπυργον σοφίαν κλεινοτάτην ἐπασκῶν ,
sections.translation: O thou that practisest most renowned high-towering wisdom ! How sweetly does a modest grace attend your words ! Happy , therefore , were they who lived in those days , in the times

sections.label: Section 1030
sections.original: τῶν προτέρων †· πρὸς τάδε σʼ ὦ κομψοπρεπῆ μοῦσαν ἔχων ,
sections.translation: of former men ! In reply , then , to these , O thou that hast a dainty-seeming Muse , it behooveth thee to say something new ; since the man has gained renown .

sections.label: Section 1034
sections.original: δεινῶν δέ σοι βουλευμάτων ἔοικε δεῖν πρὸς αὐτόν ,
sections.translation: And it appears you have need of powerful arguments against him , if you are to conquer the man and not incur laughter .

sections.label: Section 1036
sections.original: καὶ μὴν πάλαι γʼ ἐπνιγόμην τὰ σπλάγχνα κἀπεθύμουν
sections.translation: And yet I was choking in my heart , and was longing to confound all these with contrary maxims . For I have been called among the deep thinkers the worse cause on this very account , that I first contrived how

sections.label: Section 1040
sections.original: τοῖσιν νόμοις καὶ ταῖς δίκαις τἀναντίʼ ἀντιλέξαι .
sections.translation: to speak against both law and justice ; and this art is worth more than ten thousand staters , that one should choose the worse cause , and nevertheless be victorious . But mark how I will confute the system of education on which he relies , who says , in the first place , that he will not permit you to be washed with warm water .

sections.label: Section 1045
sections.original: καίτοι τίνα γνώμην ἔχων ψέγεις τὰ θερμὰ λουτρά ;
sections.translation: And yet , on what principle do you blame the warm baths ?

sections.label: Section 1046
sections.original: ὁτιὴ κάκιστόν ἐστι καὶ δειλὸν ποιεῖ τὸν ἄνδρα .
sections.translation: Because it is most vile , and makes a man cowardly .

sections.label: Section 1047
sections.original: ἐπίσχες · εὐθὺς γάρ σʼ ἔχω μέσον λαβὼν ἄφυκτον .
sections.translation: Stop ! For immediately I seize and hold you by the waist without escape . Come , tell me , which of the sons of Jupiter do you deem to have been the bravest in soul , and to have undergone most labours ?

sections.label: Section 1050
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν οὐδένʼ Ἡρακλέους βελτίονʼ ἄνδρα κρίνω .
sections.translation: I consider no man superior to Hercules .

sections.label: Section 1051
sections.original: ποῦ ψυχρὰ δῆτα πώποτʼ εἶδες Ἡράκλεια λουτρά ;
sections.translation: Where , pray , did you ever see cold Herculean baths ? And yet , who was more valiant than he ?

sections.label: Section 1052b
sections.original: ταῦτʼ ἐστὶ ταῦτʼ ἐκεῖνα ,
sections.translation: These are the very things which make the bath full of youths always chattering all day long , but the palaestras empty .

sections.label: Section 1055
sections.original: εἶτʼ ἐν ἀγορᾷ τὴν διατριβὴν ψέγεις · ἐγὼ δʼ ἐπαινῶ .
sections.translation: You next find fault with their living in the market-place ; but I commend it . For if it had been bad , Homer would never have been for representing Nestor as an orator ; nor all the other wise men . I will return , then , from thence to the tongue , which this fellow says our youths ought not to exercise , while I maintain they should .

sections.label: Section 1060
sections.original: καὶ σωφρονεῖν αὖ φησι χρῆναι · δύο κακὼ μεγίστω .
sections.translation: And again , he says they ought to be modest : two very great evils . For tell me to whom you have ever seen any good accrue through modesty and confute me by your words .

sections.label: Section 1063
sections.original: πολλοῖς . ὁ γοῦν Πηλεὺς ἔλαβε διὰ τοῦτο τὴν μάχαιραν .
sections.translation: To many . Peleus , at any rate , received his sword on account of it .

sections.label: Section 1064
sections.original: μάχαιραν ; ἀστεῖόν γε κέρδος ἔλαβεν ὁ κακοδαίμων .
sections.translation: A sword ? Marry , he got a pretty piece of luck , the poor wretch ! While Hyperbolus , he of the lamps , got more than many talents by his villainy , but by Jupiter , no sword !

sections.label: Section 1067
sections.original: καὶ τὴν Θέτιν γʼ ἔγημε διὰ τὸ σωφρονεῖν ὁ Πηλεύς .
sections.translation: And Peleus married Thetis , too , through his modesty .
